title: Quiz: Non-Markovian Quantum Dynamics
description: Test your understanding of memory effects and non-Markovian dynamics in open quantum systems

questions.question: What is the key signature of non-Markovian quantum dynamics?

options.text: Information can flow back from environment to system
The system always reaches thermal equilibrium

questions.explanation: Non-Markovian dynamics is characterized by information backflow - quantum information that leaked to the environment can return to the system. This manifests as temporary increases in coherence, distinguishability, or other quantum properties.

questions.question: In the BLP measure of non-Markovianity, what quantity is monitored?
questions.explanation: The BLP measure tracks the trace distance D(rho1, rho2) between pairs of initial states. Non-Markovianity is indicated when this distance increases (states become more distinguishable over time).

questions.question: What happens to the effective Lindblad decay rates in non-Markovian dynamics?

options.text: They oscillate at the system frequency

questions.explanation: In the time-local (TCL) master equation description of non-Markovian dynamics, the effective decay rates can become temporarily negative. This corresponds to information flowing back from the environment.

questions.question: A structured environment (like a photonic band gap) typically leads to more non-Markovian dynamics compared to a flat spectral density.
questions.explanation: True. Structured environments have longer correlation times and specific frequency responses that create memory effects. A flat spectral density leads to delta-correlated noise and Markovian dynamics.

questions.question: The Nakajima-Zwanzig equation includes what additional feature compared to the Lindblad equation?

options.text: A memory kernel integral over past history

questions.explanation: The Nakajima-Zwanzig equation includes a memory integral: int_0^t K(t-s) rho(s) ds. The memory kernel K encodes the environment correlation function and makes the evolution history-dependent.

questions.question: What does HEOM stand for in the context of non-Markovian dynamics?
questions.explanation: HEOM stands for Hierarchical Equations of Motion, a numerically exact method for simulating non-Markovian dynamics with Gaussian environments.

questions.question: Which conditions favor non-Markovian dynamics? (Select all)
questions.explanation: Strong coupling and structured environments favor non-Markovianity. High temperature and short correlation times (options c, d) lead to faster decoherence and more Markovian behavior.

questions.question: In the context of divisibility, when is a quantum dynamical map Markovian?

options.text: When Lambda(t2) = Lambda(t1) for all t2 > t1
When Lambda(t2,t1) is completely positive for all t2 > t1

questions.explanation: A Markovian map is CP-divisible: Lambda(t2) = Lambda(t2,t1) o Lambda(t1) where Lambda(t2,t1) is completely positive. Non-Markovianity occurs when the intermediate map loses complete positivity.
